text_blocks.text: For many geologic applications,
isotope ratios of radioactive elements are
measured in minerals that give the amount of
time that has passed since a rock passed
through its particular closure temperature, the
point at which different radiometric isotopes
stop diffusing into and out of the crystal
lattice. These are used in geochromologic and
thermochronologic studies. Common methods
include uranium-lead dating, potassium-argon
dating, argon-argon dating and
uranium-thorium dating. These methods are
used for a variety of applications. Dating of
lava and volcanic ash layers found within a
stratigraphic sequence can provide absolute age
data for sedimentary rock units which do not
contain radioactive isotopes and calibrate
relative dating techniques. These methods can
also be used to determine ages of pluton
emplacement. Thermochemical techniques
can be used to determine temperature profiles
within the crust, the uplift of mountain
ranges, and paleotopography.
